coredata.dc:title: Simultaneous sensing γ-glutamyl transpeptidase and alkaline phosphatase by robust dual-emission carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Tong, Xia
Zhu, Yongfeng
Tong, Chaoying
Shi, Shuyun
Long, Ruiqing
Guo, Ying
coredata.dc:description: Rapid, convenient, sensitive and simultaneous detection of distinct enzymes is urgently needed for diagnosis, therapeutics and prognostic of related diseases. Here, a new strategy for simultaneous monitoring γ-glutamyl transpeptidase (GGT) and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity has been fabricated based on dual-emission carbon dots (CDs). CDs were prepared by solvothermal treatment of Actinidia chinensis, which presents two fluorescent emissions at 471 nm (blue channel) and 671 nm (red channel). GGT and ALP activity can be detected based on inner filter effect (IFE) and static quenching effect (SQE) of blue and red channels of CDs, respectively. Linear ranges were 2.5–90 U L−1 and 5–200 U L−1, and limit of detection (LOD) were 0.71 U L−1 and 1.2 U L−1 for GGT and ALP, respectively. Developed CDs can monitor GGT and ALP activity in human serum samples with satisfied recoveries (99.3%–108.6% for GGT, 98.4%–105.4% for ALP). Furthermore, the combination of CDs to sense GGT and ALP activity with OR logic gate can predict human health status. The design and application of dual-emission CDs can also be extended as promising tools to detect multianalytes using different channel signals.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Biomass source
Dual-channel detection
γ-glutamyl transpeptidase
Alkaline phosphatase
Molecular logic gate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of GGT and ALP Activity
GGT and ALP Activity Assay in Real Samples
Fabrication and Fluorescent Mechanism of Simultaneous Assay GGT and ALP Activity
Optimization and Selectivity of GGT and ALP Activity Assay
Quantitative Assay of GGT and ALP Activity

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 31660181 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province, China ( 2018JJ1043 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image and HR-TEM image (inset) of CDs. (B) XPS survey spectrum of CDs. (C) FT-IR spectrum of CDs. (D) UV–vis absorption spectrum of CDs, fluorescence emission spectrum of CDs at 410 nm excitation, and fluorescence excitation spectra of CDs at 471 nm and 671 nm emissions, respectively.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra of γ-GPNA (170 μM) and PNA (170 μM). (B) Fluorescence lifetime of bCDs in the absence and presence PNA (170 μM). (C) Fluorescent spectra of rCDs, in the presence of PPi (200 μM)-Cu2+ (30 μM), and PPi (200 μM)-Cu2+ (30 μM) + ALP (250 U L−1). (D) Fluorescence lifetime of rCDs without and with Cu2+ (30 μM).

(A) Fluorescence responses of bCDs towards GGT activity (0–100 U L−1) in Tris-HCl buffer (10 mM, pH 8.0); (B) Linear fitting curve of (F
0
–F)/F at 471 nm versus GGT activity ranging from 2.5 to 90 U L−1; (C) The change of fluorescent spectra of rCDs to ALP activity (0–250 U L−1) in Tris-HCl buffer (10 mM, pH 8.0); (D) Linear correlation between (F
0
–F)/F 0 at 671 nm and ALP activity (5–200 U L−1). The experiments were repeated three times.
Fluorescent spectra of bCDs and rCDs mixing with different GGT and ALP activities.
(A) OR logic gate diagram for dual-emission CDs to GGT and ALP. (B) The truth table of “OR” logic gate.
Schematic representation of the synthesis and application of dual-emission CDs for simultaneous GGT and ALP activity assay.

head.ce:title: Simultaneous sensing γ-glutamyl transpeptidase and alkaline phosphatase by robust dual-emission carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha, 410083, Hunan, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha, 410083, Hunan, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Clinical Pharmacology, Xiangya Hospital, Hunan Key Laboratory of Pharmacogenetics, Central South University, Changsha, 410078, Hunan, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Clinical Pharmacology, Xiangya Hospital, Hunan Key Laboratory of Pharmacogenetics, Central South University, Changsha, 410078, Hunan, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University, Changsha, 410083, Hunan, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Rapid, convenient, sensitive and simultaneous detection of distinct enzymes is urgently needed for diagnosis, therapeutics and prognostic of related diseases. Here, a new strategy for simultaneous monitoring γ-glutamyl transpeptidase (GGT) and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) activity has been fabricated based on dual-emission carbon dots (CDs). CDs were prepared by solvothermal treatment of Actinidia chinensis, which presents two fluorescent emissions at 471 nm (blue channel) and 671 nm (red channel). GGT and ALP activity can be detected based on inner filter effect (IFE) and static quenching effect (SQE) of blue and red channels of CDs, respectively. Linear ranges were 2.5–90 U L−1 and 5–200 U L−1, and limit of detection (LOD) were 0.71 U L−1 and 1.2 U L−1 for GGT and ALP, respectively. Developed CDs can monitor GGT and ALP activity in human serum samples with satisfied recoveries (99.3%–108.6% for GGT, 98.4%–105.4% for ALP). Furthermore, the combination of CDs to sense GGT and ALP activity with OR logic gate can predict human health status. The design and application of dual-emission CDs can also be extended as promising tools to detect multianalytes using different channel signals.

ce:section.ce:para: Enzymes with fundamental functions are always regarded as biomarkers in clinical diagnosis, while abnormal enzyme activity are closely related to many diseases [1]. It is therefore an urgent need for simultaneous detection of multiple enzymes for one pathology to improve diagnostic accuracy and expedite right treatment [2]. Activity of serum γ-glutamyl transpeptidase (GGT) and alkaline phosphatase (ALP) are regarded as two significant biomarkers in biological systems [2,3]. GGT participates in the cleavage of γ-glutamyl group of endogenous glutathione (GSH) to maintain cellular redox equilibrium [4]. Overexpression of GGT is closely associated with hepatobiliary diseases, cervical and ovarian cancer [4,5]. ALP catalyzes dephosphorylation process of proteins, nucleic acids and some small molecules. Abnormal activity of ALP is related to liver dysfunction, hepatitis, breast and prostate cancer [6]. It has been reported that serum GGT and ALP activity reveal an elevation with more than one time than normal ranges in primary biliary cholangitis (PBC) [7] and cryptogenic liver disease [8]. Therefore, rapid, convenient, sensitive and simultaneous detection of serum GGT and ALP activity is urgently needed for diagnosis, therapeutics and prognostic of hepatobiliary, and cancers related diseases. Up to date, a number of methods have been developed to determinate ALP or GGT activity with excellent selectivity and high sensitivity, including but not limited to fluorometric [3,4,9,10], colorimetric [11], electrochemistry [12,13], and surface enhanced resonance Raman scattering [14,15] assays.

Fluorescent methods have attracted great attentions due to their simple operation, relatively low cost, rapid response, excellent sensitivity, high selectivity, and portability. The fluorescent materials, such as quantum dots (QDs) [16], carbon dots (CDs) [9,17,18], metal-organic framework (MOF) [19] and organic molecules [3,4,6,20], have been applied to sense ALP or GGT activity widely. CDs, a novel kind of zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, have been received extensive interests in biosensing and bioimaging for their facile preparation, unique optical properties, high stability, and excellent biocompatibility [21–25]. In our previous study, GGT activity was monitored via inner filter effect (IFE) between GGT catalyzed product (p-nitroaniline (PNA)) and nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) [9]. Liu et al. found that fluorescence intensity of CDs could be quenched by ultrathin cobalt oxyhydroxide (CoOOH) nanosheets, which could then be recovered after the reduction of CoOOH by ALP enzymatic product (ascorbic acid), and then the activity of ALP was assayed [26]. Niu et al. assayed ALP activity based on the specific competitive coordination interaction between N-CQDs and Fe3+‒pyrophosphate anions (PPi) or ALP hydrolyzed product of PPi [18]. Obviously, CDs-based sensors for ALP or GGT assay were efficient, sensitive and selective. However, to our best knowledge, they are all single-enzyme assay approaches, and no strategy has been fabricated to monitor GGT and ALP concurrently, mainly because of the difficulty in simultaneous monitoring two enzymatic reactions.

Single-emission CDs-based fluorescent assays for enzyme activity have been successfully developed by fabrication of suitable CDs‒quencher (e.g. substrate, enzymatic product, or indirect product) pair [9,10,17,18]. How about dual-emission CDs? If they can be fabricated for dual-channel differentiation of two enzymes? Recently, unique approach for preparation of biomass-based dual-emission CDs (blue emission from intrinsic structures of CDs core, and red emission from surface porphyrin groups) has been reported by one-pot solvothermal method [27–30]. Different fluorescent characteristics may result in different signal responses. It should be noted that cabbage derived dual-emission CDs can determine pH and Hg2+ at the same time using dual-channel sensing [31]. Thus, we deduce that dual-emission biomass CDs are invaluable tools to detect two enzymes conveniently and efficiently with distinct emission profiles after reasonable design.

Herein, a dual-emission CDs were prepared by solvothermal treatment of Actinidia chinensis in anhydrous ethanol solution. Obtained CDs present two fluorescent emissions at 471 nm (blue emission, bCDs, excitation dependent) and 671 nm (red emission, rCDs, excitation independent) under 410 nm excitation. Absorption spectrum of PNA (Abs. maximum 381 nm), GGT catalytic product, overlaps largely with excitation spectrum of bCDs. Thus, GGT activity can be detected by bCDs at 410 nm excitation based on IFE. ALP can selectively hydrolyze Cu2+-PPi complex to generate phosphate (Pi) and release Cu2+. Then released Cu2+ can complex with CDs to quench fluorescence of rCDs via static quenching effect (SQE). As Cu2+ coordinates with PPi stronger than CDs, ALP activity can be evaluated according to the fluorescence quenching of rCDs at 615 nm excitation. Notably, selection of appropriate excitation wavelengths is very crucial to avoid crosstalk and improve accuracy and selectivity. The rational design of CDs for simultaneous detection of GGT and ALP activity was presented in Scheme 1 . This developed strategy was successfully applied to simultaneously sense GGT and ALP activity in human serum samples. Furthermore, GGT and ALP activity were as inputs to construct an “OR” logic gate and normal (“0”) or overexpressed (“1”) as outputs to diagnose related diseases. As was known, this is the first report to sense two enzymes simultaneously based on CDs. This strategy also provides a potential method to detect multi-target analytes.

Fresh A. chinensis was washed, sliced and dried at 60 °C overnight. Dried A. chinensis (2.0 g) and anhydrous ethanol (20 mL) were mixed in a round-bottom flask (100 mL), and refluxed at 100 °C for 20 h under nitrogen protection. After being cooled to room temperature, supernatant was filtered (0.22 μm) and mixed with 1.0 mg mL−1 H2N-PEG-NH2 in 4 mL ethanol for reflux at 75 °C for 4 h. Then, CDs was acquired by dialysis (500 D) of reaction solution for 3 days and freeze-drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of GGT and ALP Activity
ce:section.ce:para: IFE-based fluorescent assay for GGT activity was performed as follows. A series of GGT with various activity ranging from 0 to 100 U L−1 was added into 1.5 mL of CDs solution (50 μg mL−1) including γ-L-glutamyl-p-nitroanilide (γ-GPNA) (170 μM) and glycylglycine (Gly-Gly) (500 μM). Reaction solution was shaken and incubated at 37 °C for 30 min and cooled to room temperature. Then, fluorescent spectra were measured at excitation wavelength of 410 nm, and scan range was set in the range of 430–600 nm. In assay of ALP activity, different activity of ALP ranging from 0–250 U L−1 was added into CDs solution (50 μg mL−1, 1.5 mL) including Mg2+ (1 mM), PPi (200 μM) and Cu2+ (30 μM). After incubation for 30 min at 37 °C and cooling to room temperature, fluorescent spectra were recorded in the range of 635–750 nm under excitation at 615 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: GGT and ALP Activity Assay in Real Samples
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the practicality of CDs, human serum samples of healthy adult volunteers and patients with PBC were obtained from Xiangya Hospital Central South University without other pretreatments. All tests were performed in compliance with the relevant laws and institutional guidelines. Experiments were approved by the Ethic Committees of Xiangya Hospital and Central South University, which conformed to the provisions of the Declaration of Helsinki. The written informed consents were acquired from healthy adult volunteers and patients. Serum samples of patients with PBC were 3-fold diluted. In GGT and ALP activity assays in human serum samples, sample (20 μL) was added into CDs solution (200 μL) including γ-GPNA (170 μM), Gly-Gly (500 μM), Cu2+ (30 μM), PPi (200 μM) and Mg2+ (1 mM). After incubation for 30 min at 37 °C, GGT and ALP activity were determined by monitoring the changes of fluorescence intensity of bCDs and rCDs, respectively.

Transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of CDs displays good dispersion (Fig. 1 A). The particle size ranges 4.0–12.5 nm with an average size of approximately 8.5 nm (Fig. S1A). According to high-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image (inset in Fig. 1A), the lattice distance in CDs is 0.21 nm, which is attributed to the (100) plane of graphite carbons. X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern (Fig. S1B) for CDs shows a broad band at 20.1° indicating amorphous nature of CDs [32]. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was applied to determine the elemental composition and chemical bonding states of CDs. As shown in Fig. 1B, full XPS survey spectrum of CDs shows three peaks at 532.42, 399.86 and 284.8 eV owing to O1s, N1s and C1s, respectively. HR-XPS of O1s exhibits two peaks at 533.0 and 532.1 eV, related to C–O and CO, respectively (Fig. S2A). In N1s spectrum, peaks at 402.3, 400.5 and 399.75 eV are assigned to N–H, graphitic-N and pyrrolic-N, respectively (Fig. S2B) [33,34]. C1s spectrum has four peaks at 288.9, 286.4, 285.1 and 284.6 eV, related to CN/CO, C–O, C–N and C–C/CC, respectively (Fig. S2C) [34]. In Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) spectrum of CDs (Fig. 1C), absorption peaks of 3310, 2914, 1694, 1590, 1351 and 1045 cm−1 are attributed to stretching vibrations of O–H/N–H, C–H, CO, CC, C–N and C–O–C, respectively [35,36]. The consistent results of XPS and FT-IR of CDs verify –COOH, –OH and N-containing functional groups (e.g. –NH2, porphyrin rings) on the surface of CDs, and lead to excellent solubility. In Fig. 1D, ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrum of CDs exhibits four absorption bands around 277, 329, 415 and 670 nm. Absorption peaks at 277 and 329 nm are ascribed to π→π∗ transition of CC bond and n→π∗ transition of CO bond in aromatic carbon, respectively. Peaks at 415 nm and 670 nm are n→π∗ transition of CO and CN bonds in chlorophyll-derived porphyrin structure, respectively [36]. Emission wavelength of CDs is at 471 nm (bCDs) and 671 nm (rCDs) at 410 nm excitation (Fig. 1D), and they may originate from carbon core and porphyrin groups on CDs. Excitation wavelength of bCDs is at 410 nm, and rCDs has excitation peaks at 415 nm, 508 nm, 539 nm and 615 nm, respectively. As shown in Fig. S3, bCDs shows excitation dependent feature with excitation wavelength increasing from 390 nm to 430 nm, while rCDs exhibits independent behavior. Quantum yields of bCDs and rCDs were 9.2% (quinine sulfate as reference) and 6.4% (Rhodamine B as reference), respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication and Fluorescent Mechanism of Simultaneous Assay GGT and ALP Activity
ce:section.ce:para: We demonstrated the feasibility of the application of dual-emission CDs to fabricate a platform to sense GGT and ALP activity simultaneously. Here, the choice of appropriate excitation wavelengths is very important. In GGT activity assay, 410 nm was selected as excitation wavelength to avoid the influence of corresponding substrate (γ-GPNA), and fluorescence of bCDs at 471 nm was recorded for evaluation of GGT activity. And at the same time, fluorescence of rCDs at 671 nm was also quenched in the presence of PNA. If excitation wavelength was set beyond absorption band of PNA (>475 nm), fluorescence of rCDs at 671 nm cannot be affected by GGT activity assay system. Then in ALP activity assay, 615 nm was selected as rational excitation wavelength.

In GGT activity assay, γ-GPNA was chosen as specific substrate, and its enzymatic product was PNA. IFE can occur when absorption spectrum of absorber overlaps with excitation or/and emission spectrum of fluorophore. Obviously, large overlap between absorption spectrum of PNA (Abs. maximum 381 nm) and excitation spectrum of bCDs can trigger IFE and results in fluorescence quenching of bCDs (Figs. 2A and 1 D) [9]. And then bCDs has been selected to determine GGT activity. The maximum absorption wavelength of γ-GPNA is at 318 nm, and it has little effect to fluorescent signal of bCDs when excitation wavelength is set at 410 nm (Fig. S4A). The average fluorescence lifetime of bCDs is fitted to be 2.77 ns and it remains unchanged after adding with GGT reaction solution (Fig. 2B). While, little overlapping of emission spectrum of bCDs and absorption spectrum of PNA also provides evidence to rule out FRET process [10]. According to Parker model [37], it is found that more than half of fluorescence quenching efficiency derives from IFE of PNA for bCDs (Fig. S4B, Table S1). Thus, IFE between bCDs and PNA happens for GGT activity determination.

Surface porphyrin groups of CDs can selectively chelate with metal ions (e.g., Hg2+, Cu2+) to form non-fluorescent complex and induce fluorescence quenching of rCDs [30]. Based on these, ALP activity assay can be designed. PPi (ALP substrate) coordinates with Cu2+ to form PPi-Cu2+ complex, and it is more stable than CDs-Cu2+ complex. In the presence of ALP, PPi-Cu2+ is catalyzed into Pi (not binding with Cu2+) and Cu2+. Then, the free Cu2+ can quench the fluorescence of rCDs with 615 nm excitation (Fig. 2C). According to Stern-Volmer equation (Fig. S4C) [38], F
0
/F displays linear relationship with Cu2+ concentration (F
0
/F = 0.0342c
q + 1.0039). In Fig. 2D, the fluorescence lifetime of rCDs is almost unchanged after introducing Cu2+ into rCDs system. According to quenching rate constant (K
q 9.40 × 1012 M−1S−1), it is considered that this quenching process is SQE rather than dynamic quenching effect (K
q less than 1.00 × 1010 M−1S−1). The quenching constant K
SV of Cu2+ for rCDs at 15 °C, 25 °C, 35 °C have been calculated, respectively. The K
SV reduces with the increment of temperature due to decline of stability of ground-state complex of rCDs-Cu2+ (Fig. S4C) [10]. In addition, the absorption peak of CDs at 670 nm appears blue-shift to 653 nm in the presence of Cu2+ indicating the formation of non-fluorescent CDs-Cu2+ complex (Fig. S4D). Therefore, this quenching process is considered as SQE. As shown in Fig. S4E, the influence of Cu2+ and PNA can be ignored to fluorescent signals of bCDs and rCDs, respectively. Thus, the feasibility of simultaneous determination of GGT and ALP activity by dual-emission of CDs is independent, and crosstalk influence does not exist.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization and Selectivity of GGT and ALP Activity Assay
ce:section.ce:para: To achieve better response for enzyme activity, synthesis conditions of CDs were optimized. The rational dual-emissive fluorescence intensity of CDs is acquired when CDs were synthesized by A. chinensis (2 g) in ethanol (20 mL) at 100 °C for 20 h under nitrogen protection (Fig. S5). To obtain the optimized response of GGT and ALP activity, concentration of CDs, solution pH, reaction temperature and incubation time were investigated (Fig. S6). In GGT assay, when concentration of CDs was 50 μg mL−1 in pH 7.4 or 8.0 at 37 °C for 30 min, fluorescence intensity of bCDs and fluorescence quenching efficiency reaches the maximum. As shown in Table S2 for ALP assay, fluorescence quenching efficiency has reached 67% with the concentration of Cu2+ at 30 μM, and at this time, PPi (200 μM) can result in remarkable fluorescence recovery. Thus, the concentration of Cu2+ and PPi were selected as 30 μM and 200 μM for further experiment. Then, in order to comprehensive consideration of dual-emission detection for GGT and ALP activity, concentration of CDs was set at 50 μg mL−1, Tris-HCl buffer (10 mM, pH 8.0) at 37 °C for 30 min were chosen as optimal enzymatic conditions.

The biological substances (such as ions, anions, amino acids and enzymes) were employed to test selectivity of CDs to GGT and ALP activity. Fig. S7A displays fluorescence response of bCDs to GGT activity in the presence of biological substances, and they have negligible interferes on GGT reaction. As shown in Fig. S7B, common ions, anions, amino acids and enzymes were tested their specificity to ALP, and they have weak influence on detection of ALP activity. To test the stability of CDs at blue and red channel, the fluorescence intensity of bCDs and rCDs was recorded under different NaCl concentration, pH and UV light irradiation. In Fig. S8A−C, the fluorescence intensity of bCDs and rCDs keeps unchanged under environmental variations, which indicates the outstanding stability of CDs. The excellent selectivity of CDs to sense GGT and ALP instead of other enzymes are attributed to specific recognition of γ-glutamyl group in γ-GPNA and phosphoester bond in PPi, respectively. Overall, outstanding sensitivity of CDs makes commercial application for GGT and ALP assay in practice.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantitative Assay of GGT and ALP Activity
ce:section.ce:para:
Firstly, we tested the feasibility of bCDs to detect PNA, which is GGT enzymatic product. The fluorescent spectra of bCDs decrease with increasing PNA concentration and show linear response ranging 1–38 μM (Fig. S9 and Table S3). Thus, it is reasonable to assay GGT activity via IFE between bCDs and PNA. As shown in Fig. 3 A, fluorescence intensity of bCDs system declines gradually with increment of GGT activity from 0 to 100 U L−1. A good linear relationship between fluorescence quenching efficiency ((F
0
–F)/F
0 ) and GGT activity is obtained in the range of 2.5–90 U L−1 (Fig. 3B). The fitting equation can be presented as y = 0.0060x+0.032 with a correlation coefficient (R
2 ) of 0.9984. Limit of detection (LOD) of this strategy to monitor GGT activity is 0.71 U L−1 (signal-to-noise ratio (S/N) of 3).

As shown in Fig. S10, fluorescence intensity of rCDs reduces gradually with increment of Cu2+ concentration, and displays good linear ranging in 0.2–30 μM (Table S4). The fluorescence intensity of rCDs-Cu2+ can be recovered gradually after addition of PPi, and shows linear relationship with PPi concentration in the range of 4–100 μM (Table S5). Then, ALP activity was studied by rCDs-Cu2+-PPi detection system. As displayed in Fig. 3C, when ALP activity from 0 to 250 U L−1 is added into reaction system, fluorescence intensity decreases gradually. Fig. 3D presents a good linear relationship between fluorescence quenching efficiency ((F
0
–F)/F
0 ) and ALP activity in range of 5–200 U L−1 (y = 0.0023x+0.022, R
2 0.9951). LOD of this method is 1.2 U L−1 (S/N 3). Furthermore, bCDs and rCDs-based techniques to monitor GGT and ALP activity has comparable linear detection range and LOD with reported detection methods (Table S6). As shown in Fig. 4 , GGT and ALP activity can be detected simultaneously in the same reaction system because the emission of bCDs and rCDs is relatively independent under different excitations.

Human serum samples were used to verify the feasibility of this method for simultaneous determination of GGT and ALP activity. As displayed in Table S7, detection results of dual-emission CDs are grossly agreed with absorbance assay methods for GGT and ALP activity. Moreover, different activity of GGT and ALP were spiked into serum samples to determine recoveries and relative standard deviation (RSD) using dual-emission CDs. The recoveries of GGT and ALP assay range from 99.3% to 108.6%, 98.4%–105.4%, and RSDs for GGT and ALP assay are 0.5%–3.5% and 0.3%–1.8%. It suggests that dual-channel CDs have great potential to monitor GGT and ALP activity in human serum samples.

Owing to the excellent performance of dual-emission CDs, an advanced OR logic gate device can be established by combination of GGT and ALP activity to predict health status (Fig. 5 A). GGT activity ranges 5–85.0 U L−1 in healthy adult, and maximum quenching efficiency for bCDs is 0.54 as threshold value. For GGT, “0” (<0.54) and “1” (>0.54) are defined as normal and overexpressed GGT activity in human serum, respectively. The normal value of ALP activity is 40–150 U L−1 in human serum. When ALP activity exceeds 150 U L−1, quenching efficiency of rCDs-Cu2+-PPi system is upper 0.37 as threshold value. For ALP, small (<0.37) and large (>0.37) value are defined as “0” and “1”, respectively. For output of OR gate, the “1” state respects GGT or/and ALP activity are beyond normal levels in human serum. While, “0” denotes that both of them maintain normal level. As truth table shown in Fig. 5B, no input (0, 0) results “0”, while output is as “1” owing to single input (1, 0), (0, 1) or both inputs (1, 1) in OR gate. Therefore, the combination of dual-emission CDs with OR gate for GGT and ALP assay are established successfully, and it can be employed for tentative diagnosis of related diseases in clinical tests.

In summary, a dual-emission CDs has been synthesized through one-step solvothermal treatment of A. chinensis. GGT and ALP activity are monitored based on IFE and SQE of blue channel and red channel of CDs, respectively. Dual-emission CDs exhibits high sensitivity and excellent selectivity for GGT and ALP assays, and it can sense GGT and ALP activity simultaneously in the same serum samples. In addition, GGT and ALP activity are as inputs, and OR logic gate is established by switching output between “0” and “1” to evaluate normal or abnormal health state. Dual-emission CDs has comparable performance with reported approaches, and provides high-throughput, shorten time and more significant reference to diagnose diseases than single assay method. This fluorescent CDs-based method has enormous potential for comprehensive, accurate, and informative studies especially for human health monitoring. Also, it offers a promising platform for multiplex enzymes detection via reasonable design strategies.

Xia Tong: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Yongfeng Zhu: Conceptualization, Methodology. Chaoying Tong: Methodology, Investigation. Shuyun Shi: Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition. Ruiqing Long: Conceptualization, Formal analysis. Ying Guo: Writing – review & editing, Resources.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31660181), the Natural Science Foundation of Hunan Province, China (2018JJ1043).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is the Supplementary data to this article:

Supporting Information Electronic supplementary information (ESI) available: materials and instruments, selective sensing of CDs, quantum yield measurements, colorimetric assay of GGT and ALP activity, supplemental spectra figures (Fig. S1−S10) and Tables (Table S1−S7).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2021.338829.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A two-photon fluorescent sensor revealing drug-induced liver injury via tracking γ-glutamyltranspeptidase (GGT) level in vivo
Biomaterials
